author: @vintagesirvivor
text:
What do you mean by "slow" ?  For example, how long would it take to print a replica coke can?  What do you mean by quiet?  Is it louder than a computer fan? Or a household fan? How loud are the loudest 3D printers?  As loud as a leaf blower?  
Also I went to their website and searched for the MD-1000 Pro.  "did not yield any results " ???

author: @internetmail3888
text: Hi Robert please give a shout to the charity called enable that has a global team of 3d printing volunteers who help design or just print out prosthetics for kids all over the world who cannot afford a prosthetic. Please get involved even if it is just volunteering your printer for use

author: @VeniceInventors
text: It makes the two Elegoo printers next to it look small. It's big enough to print cowboy hats, rain boots, some musical instruments, and most importantly... Some sizeable turbines and windmills!

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: as a chemist it would be great if you could cooperate with one of these 3D printer companies to create some enzymatic biopolymer from a genetically modified yeast, that can be coded into it's enzymatic process, that is both enivonrmentally friendly and even edible to organisms but firm enough to print usable objects with, rather than being environmentally harmful polymers. As you may know, hyaluronic acid, just as an example, is a critical component in the faschia, a critical component in the human body in the extracellular matrix of all cells,  is a biopolymer: "Hyaluronic acid (hyaluronan, HA) is a linear polysaccharide formed from disaccharide units containing N-acetyl-D-glucosamine and glucuronic acid". However I'm talking about a more firm biopolymer that could be used in environmentally friendly printing, but could be wholly edible by species of plants or higher fungi (i.e. non-molds, excuse the pun). So I am not sure if you could invent, as a chemist, some firm type of biopolymer the molecular design of which could then be impressed onto, i.e. coded into the enzymatic processes of certain species of yeast which would excrete this polymer, which you'd need some molecular biologists assistance with rather than continuing the use of fossil fuels, none of which exist, as you may know, on any other moons or planets in the solar system AS FAR AS WE KNOW... My point is, will we TAKE fossil fuels into space to create required polymers, or will we MAKE polymers in space? Through biomechanical processes? Obviously I feel I have my answer.

author: @TheWorldBelow360
text: Hey Robert! Will you plz print something that won’t turn critical after being assembled in a vacuum?! I want a helium ball to see if it wants to escape.

replies.author: @VeniceInventors
replies.text: I thought about that one, making spheres with a vacuum inside. After looking at the options I'm aware of, I came to the conclusion that any material strong enough to resist the air pressure around it is going to weigh a lot more than the volume of air displaced, at least at a marble to tennis ball scale. At a much larger scale it may be that buoyancy can be achieved because the volume increases about twice faster than the surface of the sphere

replies.author: @TheWorldBelow360
replies.text:
@VeniceInventors 
I reached the same. But at high enough altitude, maybe heating the helium would drive it insane. And a reasonable floating greenhouse might actually maintain.
